subtopic_detail: Endometriosis empirical COCP trial then diagnostic laparoscopy
stem: You are the general practice registrar reviewing a 28-year-old nulliparous woman with 18 months of progressive cyclical pelvic pain, deep dyspareunia and dysmenorrhoea unresponsive to escalating doses of paracetamol and ibuprofen. She is not currently trying to conceive and uses condoms for contraception. There is no abnormal vaginal discharge, no urinary symptoms, and she has had a negative chlamydia and gonorrhoea screen 3 months ago. Speculum and bimanual examination today are unremarkable. Transvaginal ultrasound shows no endometrioma, no obvious deep infiltrating endometriosis nodule and a normal uterus. She is interested in symptom management but does not wish to conceive in the next 12 months.

data_table.Examination: Soft non-tender abdomen, bimanual exam unremarkable, no adnexal mass or tenderness, normal cervix on speculum exam
data_table.Investigations: Transvaginal ultrasound: normal uterus, no endometrioma, no deep infiltrating endometriosis nodule, ovaries of normal volume; recent chlamydia and gonorrhoea NAAT negative; cervical screening up to date and negative

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Commence empirical combined oral contraceptive pill taken continuously without a withdrawal week for 3 months as a therapeutic trial for suspected endometriosis, with planned review and consideration of diagnostic laparoscopy if symptoms fail to improve substantially
options.rationale: Empirical hormonal therapy with continuous COCP is first-line for suspected endometriosis; diagnostic laparoscopy is reserved for symptoms failing medical therapy or fertility concerns. (Source: RANZCOG: Endometriosis)

options.text: Refer for diagnostic and operative laparoscopy as first-line management for all women with symptoms suggesting endometriosis, given the limitations of transvaginal ultrasound in identifying superficial peritoneal disease and the gold-standard role of laparoscopy
options.rationale: Empirical medical therapy is first-line; laparoscopy is offered if medical therapy fails, fertility is a concern, or there are red flags such as endometrioma. (Source: RANZCOG: Endometriosis)

options.text: Arrange a contrast pelvic magnetic resonance imaging to characterise deep infiltrating endometriosis prior to commencing any therapy, with planned multidisciplinary case conference to map disease distribution before considering hormonal management options
options.rationale: Pelvic MRI is not routine initial imaging for suspected endometriosis without deep infiltrating disease on TVUS; it does not change first-line empirical management. (Source: RANZCOG: Endometriosis)

options.text: Commence gonadotropin-releasing hormone analogue therapy with goserelin 3.6 mg subcutaneously monthly with add-back hormone therapy to minimise bone density loss and vasomotor symptoms, with planned 6-month course as first-line medical management
options.rationale: GnRH analogues with add-back are second or third-line due to cost, side effects and need for add-back; not first-line empirical therapy. (Source: RANZCOG: Endometriosis)

options.text: Prescribe oxycodone immediate release 5 mg orally up to four times daily as required for chronic cyclical pelvic pain with planned chronic pain clinic referral for opioid stewardship and review of escalation criteria for intervention
options.rationale: Chronic opioid therapy for suspected endometriosis without medical therapy or diagnosis is inappropriate; first-line is hormonal suppression. (Source: RANZCOG: Endometriosis)

explanation.summary: Clinically suspected endometriosis based on cyclical pelvic pain, dysmenorrhoea and deep dyspareunia is managed empirically with first-line hormonal suppression of ovarian cycling: continuous combined oral contraceptive pill or progestogen-only options including a levonorgestrel-releasing IUD, desogestrel, or dienogest. Diagnostic laparoscopy is reserved for women with symptoms failing first-line therapy, suspected deep infiltrating disease, endometrioma, or fertility concerns. Pelvic MRI is added when deep infiltrating endometriosis is suspected on examination or ultrasound. GnRH analogues with add-back are reserved for resistant cases.
key_points: Continuous COCP or progestogen is first-line empirical therapy
Diagnostic laparoscopy reserved for failed medical therapy or fertility concern
Levonorgestrel IUD and dienogest are progestogen alternatives
explanation.pearls: Cyclical pelvic pain that fails escalating hormonal suppression should prompt referral to a gynaecology multidisciplinary endometriosis service for laparoscopic mapping and consideration of excisional surgery and pain medicine input.

subtopic_detail: MCDA twin selective IUGR type II reversed end-diastolic flow delivery timing
stem: You are the maternal foetal medicine fellow reviewing a 30-year-old G1P0 woman at 34 plus 2 weeks gestation with a monochorionic diamniotic twin pregnancy. Twin A has been growing on the 50th centile throughout pregnancy. Twin B has been on the 5th centile since 28 weeks with an estimated foetal weight discrepancy of 28% compared to twin A. Today twin B has intermittently reversed end-diastolic flow in the umbilical artery. There is no evidence of twin-to-twin transfusion syndrome (deepest vertical pockets normal in both sacs) and no evidence of foetal anaemia on middle cerebral artery Doppler. Both foetuses have reactive CTGs at this time.

data_table.Examination: Symphysis-fundal height appropriate for gestation, no tenderness, no contractions, normal abdominal examination, foetal heart auscultation audible from both sacs
data_table.Investigations: MCDA twins; growth scan today: twin A 2350 g (50th centile), twin B 1690 g (less than 5th centile); twin B umbilical artery Doppler with intermittent reversed end-diastolic flow consistent with type II selective IUGR; twin A umbilical Doppler normal; both MCA peak systolic velocities normal; deepest vertical pocket twin A 5 cm, twin B 3 cm; both CTGs reactive; betamethasone given at 32 weeks for prior threatened preterm labour

lead_in: What is the most appropriate management plan?

options.text: Plan elective lower segment caesarean section within 24 hours with neonatal team standby for a high-risk preterm twin delivery, repeat magnesium sulfate for foetal neuroprotection at 34 weeks, and admit to the foetal medicine ward for monitoring overnight
options.rationale: Type II selective IUGR with intermittent or persistent absent or reversed end-diastolic flow at 32 weeks or beyond is an indication for delivery with neonatal team standby; caesarean is preferred for monochorionic discordant twins. (Source: RANZCOG: Multiple Pregnancy)

options.text: Continue weekly outpatient surveillance with growth scans, umbilical artery and ductus venosus Doppler, and twice-weekly CTG monitoring with delivery deferred until 37 weeks gestation to maximise foetal lung maturity prior to elective delivery of the twins
options.rationale: Continued expectant management beyond 34 weeks with reversed end-diastolic flow risks demise of twin B and co-twin injury; delivery is indicated. (Source: RANZCOG: Multiple Pregnancy)

options.text: Offer foetoscopic laser ablation of placental anastomoses to selectively reduce twin B given the marked growth discrepancy and abnormal Doppler, to protect the larger twin A from neurological injury associated with intrauterine demise of the smaller twin
options.rationale: Laser ablation is used in twin-to-twin transfusion syndrome (Quintero stage II or above) at earlier gestations, not for selective IUGR at 34 weeks. (Source: RANZCOG: Multiple Pregnancy)

options.text: Arrange intrauterine red cell transfusion to twin B given the abnormal umbilical artery Doppler suggestive of foetal anaemia and growth restriction, with cross-matched O negative blood and ultrasound-guided percutaneous umbilical sampling immediately
options.rationale: Reversed end-diastolic flow reflects placental insufficiency, not anaemia; MCA Doppler is normal here, so intrauterine transfusion is not indicated. (Source: RANZCOG: Multiple Pregnancy)

options.text: Administer a single intramuscular betamethasone rescue dose given that the previous course was given more than 2 weeks ago, and plan delivery at 36 plus 0 weeks gestation if the umbilical Doppler does not deteriorate further on weekly review
options.rationale: Rescue betamethasone alone does not address the foetal indication for delivery; reversed end-diastolic flow at 34 weeks mandates timely delivery. (Source: RANZCOG: Multiple Pregnancy)

explanation.summary: Selective intrauterine growth restriction in monochorionic diamniotic twins is classified by the umbilical artery Doppler pattern of the smaller twin: type I (positive end-diastolic flow), type II (persistent absent or reversed end-diastolic flow), and type III (intermittent absent or reversed end-diastolic flow). Type II from 32 weeks onwards typically prompts delivery; type III is more unpredictable but generally delivered by 34 weeks. Caesarean is preferred for monochorionic discordant twin delivery due to risks of intrapartum hypoxia. Magnesium sulfate for foetal neuroprotection is given for preterm delivery below 30 weeks routinely.
key_points: Type II selective IUGR (persistent absent/reversed flow) usually delivered from 32 weeks
Type III (intermittent reversed flow) typically delivered by 34 weeks
Caesarean preferred for monochorionic discordant twin delivery
explanation.pearls: Intrauterine demise of one monochorionic twin carries 18-25% risk of co-twin neurological injury due to acute fetal-fetal haemorrhage through placental anastomoses, regardless of how rapidly delivery occurs after demise.

subtopic_detail: Antipsychotic metabolic monitoring at 12 weeks on olanzapine
stem: You are the community mental health team registrar reviewing a 28-year-old man who has been on olanzapine 10 mg nocte for 12 weeks for a first episode of schizophrenia. He is settled mentally, has good adherence and minimal residual psychotic symptoms. Since starting treatment he has gained 6 kg in weight and reports daytime sedation, increased appetite and reduced exercise. His baseline 12 weeks ago showed normal weight, normal fasting glucose, normal lipid panel and a normal ECG. He has no chest pain, no orthostatic symptoms, no galactorrhoea and no gynaecomastia. He has no past medical history and is not on any other regular medications.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 128/82, HR 78, BMI 27 (baseline 23), waist circumference 96 cm (up 8 cm)
data_table.Examination: Settled affect, no extrapyramidal side effects, no orthostatic hypotension, normal cardiovascular and abdominal examination, no obvious tardive dyskinesia
data_table.Investigations: Baseline 12 weeks ago: fasting glucose 4.9 normal, lipids normal, ECG QTc 410 ms, prolactin not done

lead_in: What is the most appropriate panel of monitoring investigations to request at this 12-week review?

options.text: HbA1c alone as the most sensitive marker for olanzapine-related glucose dysregulation, given that fasting glucose may miss postprandial hyperglycaemia in early metabolic disturbance and is more practical in the community mental health setting
options.rationale: HbA1c alone is insufficient; the recommended panel is fasting glucose (or HbA1c), fasting lipid profile, weight, BMI and waist circumference at 12 weeks. (Source: RANZCP: Antipsychotic Monitoring Guidelines)

options.text: Thyroid function tests including TSH free T4 free T3 and thyroid antibodies given the weight gain, sedation and possible iatrogenic hypothyroidism related to olanzapine treatment, and assessment of any underlying thyroid contribution to ongoing symptoms
options.rationale: Thyroid testing is not indicated as routine antipsychotic monitoring without specific clinical features of thyroid disease; metabolic panel is the priority. (Source: RANZCP: Antipsychotic Monitoring Guidelines)

options.text: Fasting glucose or HbA1c, fasting lipid profile, body weight, BMI and waist circumference, with consideration of metformin and clozapine review if metabolic parameters have deteriorated significantly compared to the baseline values taken before olanzapine commencement
options.rationale: RANZCP and Maudsley antipsychotic metabolic monitoring at 12 weeks comprises fasting glucose or HbA1c, fasting lipid panel, weight, BMI and waist circumference. (Source: RANZCP: Antipsychotic Monitoring Guidelines)

options.text: Serum prolactin level alone as the primary monitoring test given that olanzapine carries a class warning for hyperprolactinaemia and patients may not volunteer sexual dysfunction or galactorrhoea symptoms without specific prompting at review consultations
options.rationale: Olanzapine is not a high-prolactin antipsychotic; routine prolactin is for prolactin-elevating agents such as risperidone or amisulpride or when clinically symptomatic. (Source: RANZCP: Antipsychotic Monitoring Guidelines)

options.text: Repeat 12-lead ECG for assessment of QTc prolongation as the highest priority safety check on antipsychotic therapy at this stage, given that 12 weeks corresponds to the typical timeframe for delayed cardiac repolarisation changes to manifest fully
options.rationale: Olanzapine is low QTc risk; routine repeat ECG is not standard antipsychotic monitoring at 12 weeks unless QTc-prolonging factors are present. (Source: RANZCP: Antipsychotic Monitoring Guidelines)

explanation.summary: Antipsychotic metabolic monitoring at the 12-week mark of treatment in Australia follows the RANZCP and Maudsley guidance: fasting glucose or HbA1c, fasting lipid profile, body weight, body mass index, and waist circumference. Olanzapine and clozapine are the highest-risk second-generation agents for weight gain, dyslipidaemia and glucose dysregulation. If metabolic parameters deteriorate beyond a 5% increase in weight or new metabolic syndrome criteria, options include lifestyle support, metformin add-on, and switching to lower-risk antipsychotics such as aripiprazole. Prolactin and ECG are reserved for specific agent profiles or clinical features.
key_points: 12-week panel: fasting glucose or HbA1c, fasting lipids, weight, BMI, waist circumference
Olanzapine and clozapine are highest metabolic risk; aripiprazole lowest
Metformin add-on indicated for weight gain greater than 5% on antipsychotic
explanation.pearls: RANZCP suggests metabolic monitoring at baseline, 6 weeks, 12 weeks, 6 months and annually thereafter; consider switching agent if BMI rises by 1 kg/m^2 or other metabolic criteria emerge.

subtopic_detail: Tricyclic antidepressant overdose sodium bicarbonate for QRS prolongation
stem: You are the emergency department registrar managing a 19-year-old woman brought in 2 hours after ingesting 50 tablets of her grandmother's amitriptyline 25 mg as a deliberate self-poisoning. She is drowsy but rouses to voice. Her airway is patent and she is breathing spontaneously. A 12-lead ECG taken now shows sinus tachycardia at 122 with a QRS duration of 130 ms, a terminal R wave of 4 mm in aVR, and no other ischaemic features. There is no significant past medical history, no allergies, and no other co-ingestants identified or admitted. Activated charcoal was not given as ingestion was more than an hour ago. Senior emergency consultant and clinical toxicologist have been called.

data_table.Vital signs: T 36.8, HR 122, BP 92/58, RR 16, SpO2 97% room air, GCS 13 (E3 V4 M6), bedside glucose 5.6 mmol/L
data_table.Examination: Drowsy but rousable, dilated pupils 6 mm bilaterally reacting sluggishly, dry mucous membranes, hypoactive bowel sounds, no clonus, no focal neurology
data_table.Investigations: 12-lead ECG: sinus tachycardia 122, QRS 130 ms, terminal R wave 4 mm in aVR; VBG pH 7.32 pCO2 38 HCO3 18; potassium 3.4, sodium 138, creatinine 78

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate management?

options.text: Administer intravenous physostigmine 2 mg slowly with continuous cardiac monitoring to reverse the central and peripheral anticholinergic features, with atropine immediately available at the bedside to manage any clinically significant bradycardia
options.rationale: Physostigmine is contraindicated in TCA overdose because it can precipitate asystole or seizures; sodium bicarbonate is the antidote. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Toxicology: TCA Overdose)

options.text: Activated charcoal 50 g via nasogastric tube to bind the remaining tricyclic antidepressant in the gastrointestinal tract, with intubation prior to administration if the patient's Glasgow Coma Scale falls further during preparation for the procedure
options.rationale: Activated charcoal beyond 1-2 hours post-ingestion has limited benefit and risks aspiration; cardiac stabilisation with sodium bicarbonate is the priority. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Toxicology: TCA Overdose)

options.text: Administer intravenous sodium bicarbonate 8.4% 1 to 2 mmol/kg as a bolus aiming to narrow the QRS and alkalinise serum pH to 7.45 to 7.55, intubate and ventilate to support pH if needed, continuous cardiac monitoring and intensive care admission
options.rationale: Sodium bicarbonate 1-2 mmol/kg is the antidote for TCA-induced QRS prolongation and hypotension; aim for pH 7.45-7.55 with narrowing of QRS as the endpoint. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Toxicology: TCA Overdose)

options.text: Commence intravenous flumazenil 0.2 mg titrated to GCS improvement, with continuous cardiac monitoring during administration and observation in resuscitation for 4 hours, alongside intravenous fluids to support blood pressure and reverse the anticholinergic syndrome
options.rationale: Flumazenil is contraindicated in mixed overdose where TCA is involved due to seizure risk and has no role in TCA toxicity. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Toxicology: TCA Overdose)

options.text: Commence haemodialysis urgently with vascular access via right internal jugular vein quad-lumen catheter to clear the tricyclic antidepressant from the systemic circulation, with continuous cardiac monitoring and intensive care admission for the procedure
options.rationale: TCAs have a large volume of distribution and high protein binding so are not effectively cleared by haemodialysis; sodium bicarbonate is the antidote. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines Toxicology: TCA Overdose)

explanation.summary: Tricyclic antidepressant overdose causes cardiotoxicity through sodium channel blockade leading to QRS prolongation, terminal R wave in aVR, hypotension and arrhythmias. Sodium bicarbonate 1-2 mmol/kg bolus is the antidote, working through both sodium loading and serum alkalinisation (target pH 7.45-7.55). The endpoint is QRS narrowing to under 100 ms. Intubation may be needed to maintain controlled ventilation and pH. Activated charcoal is reserved for ingestions within 1-2 hours of presentation; flumazenil and physostigmine are contraindicated; haemodialysis does not work due to high volume of distribution.
key_points: Sodium bicarbonate 1-2 mmol/kg for QRS over 100 ms or hypotension; target pH 7.45-7.55
Endpoint is QRS narrowing under 100 ms
Flumazenil and physostigmine contraindicated; haemodialysis ineffective
explanation.pearls: Persistent hypotension despite sodium bicarbonate is treated with intravenous lipid emulsion 20% as a 1.5 mL/kg bolus and consideration of intra-aortic balloon pump or VA-ECMO in severe refractory cases.

subtopic_detail: Borderline personality disorder DBT first-line outpatient referral
stem: You are the general practice registrar reviewing a 24-year-old woman with five emergency department presentations over the past 8 months for recurrent superficial cutting and overdose threats following relationship conflicts. She describes a pervasive sense of emptiness, marked identity disturbance, intense unstable interpersonal relationships, and impulsive episodes of binge eating, spending and sexual activity. Her affect varies sharply over hours. She has been on three different antidepressants and a low-dose antipsychotic over the last 2 years with no sustained benefit. She has no current intent or plan to end her life today, and has agreed to engage with outpatient mental health services for a sustainable longer-term plan.

data_table.Examination: Multiple superficial linear scars on the forearms in different stages of healing, no fresh wounds, mildly tearful affect, clear sensorium, no thought disorder, no psychotic symptoms, no current suicidal ideation
data_table.Investigations: FBC, UEC, LFTs, TFTs all within normal limits; urine drug screen negative; ECG normal

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step in management?

options.text: Increase her current sertraline to 200 mg daily with addition of quetiapine 100 mg nocte for affective instability and impulsivity, with weekly general practice review for the first month and mental health care plan referral for supportive counselling
options.rationale: Pharmacotherapy is not first-line for core borderline personality disorder symptoms; structured psychotherapy such as DBT is first-line per RANZCP guidance. (Source: RANZCP: Personality Disorders)

options.text: Admit voluntarily to an acute psychiatric inpatient unit for 4 to 6 weeks to provide containment and intensive day-long therapy with daily group work and individual sessions, with discharge planning back to the community mental health team
options.rationale: Prolonged psychiatric admission for borderline personality disorder is associated with worse outcomes and regression; outpatient structured psychotherapy is preferred. (Source: RANZCP: Personality Disorders)

options.text: Initiate weekly cognitive behavioural therapy alone delivered through a 10-session mental health care plan with the general practitioner, given the high prevalence of cognitive distortions and maladaptive coping strategies underpinning the personality presentation
options.rationale: Standard CBT is less evidence-based for borderline personality disorder than dialectical behaviour therapy or other structured personality-disorder-specific therapies. (Source: RANZCP: Personality Disorders)

options.text: Refer to a community-based dialectical behaviour therapy programme as first-line treatment with planned 12-month duration involving weekly individual therapy, weekly skills group and between-session coaching, with general practice mental health care plan as the funding pathway
options.rationale: Dialectical behaviour therapy is first-line evidence-based treatment for borderline personality disorder per RANZCP and NHMRC, reducing self-harm and ED presentations over 12 months. (Source: RANZCP: Personality Disorders)

options.text: Discharge to general practice care only with planned mental health care plan and supportive counselling, given that personality disorders typically do not respond to specialist mental health input and management is primarily through chronic disease care frameworks
options.rationale: Borderline personality disorder is treatable with structured psychotherapy; declining specialist referral underestimates evidence base for therapeutic response. (Source: RANZCP: Personality Disorders)

explanation.summary: Borderline personality disorder is treatable, with structured psychotherapy as first-line care according to RANZCP and NHMRC guidance. Dialectical behaviour therapy (Linehan), with weekly individual therapy, weekly skills group, between-session coaching and therapist consultation team, has the strongest evidence base, reducing self-harm, emergency presentations and hospitalisation over 12-month courses. Other evidence-based therapies include mentalisation-based therapy, schema therapy, and transference-focused psychotherapy. Pharmacotherapy is not first-line and is reserved for short-term management of comorbid depression, anxiety or psychotic features.
key_points: DBT is first-line for borderline personality disorder
Pharmacotherapy is adjunctive, not core treatment
Prolonged inpatient admission worsens outcomes; outpatient structured psychotherapy preferred
explanation.pearls: Brief crisis admissions of 1 to 3 days for acute risk are acceptable but should be structured with a clear discharge plan back to outpatient psychotherapy to avoid iatrogenic regression.

sources.label: NHMRC: Clinical Practice Guideline for the Management of Borderline Personality Disorder
